coredata.dc:title: Carbon Dot-Driven phase transition in coaxial Wet-Spun PVDF fibers for wearable piezoelectric energy harvesting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jun
Yue, Tiantian
Wang, Mengqi
Liu, Yi
Liao, Fan
Kang, Zhenhui
Liu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: Flexible piezoelectrics are pivotal for self-powered wearable systems, yet low-dimensional materials like PVDF fibers suffer from inefficient β-phase crystallization and limited charge transport. Here, coaxial wet-spun polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) fibers integrated with carbon quantum dots (CDs) overcome these limitations via solvent-nonsolvent phase separation and mechanical stretching. A 3 wt% CDs doping induces 75.96 % β-phase content through interfacial hydrogen bonding, achieving a high piezoelectric coefficient (d33 of 28 pC/N) and output performance of 19.4 V and 0.2 μA, which is 86.7 % surpassing pristine PVDF(15 pC/N), and Under optimal experimental conditions, the peak voltage and current can reach up to 31 V and 0.2 μA. Cyclic durability tests (3 Hz, 20 N, 1000 cycles) confirm excellent stability with > 90 % performance retention. Practical applications highlight rapid capacitor charging (5 V in 30 s) and the ability to power nine LEDs. Additionally, bending sensitivity (3.6 V at 60°) and motion sensing (9 V for arm bending) validate its adaptability for wearable electronics and IoT applications, offering a scalable strategy for high-performance flexible energy harvesters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
PVDF fiber
Wet spinning
Wearable energy harvester
Piezoelectrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and Packaging of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensors
Morphological and structural analysis of PVDF/CDs composite fibers
Analysis of the piezoelectric performance of PVDF/CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process and mechanism of PVDF/CDs.

Synthesis and characterization of PVDF/CDs: (a-c) SEM images (3 wt% CDs) at 100 μm, 10 μm, and 500 nm scales; (d) FTIR spectra; (e) XRD patterns; (f) DSC curves (varying CDs content); (g) 2D-WAXD profile; and (h-i) XPS spectra of O 1 s and C 1 s for 3 wt% CDs.

Electromechanical characterization of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensors: (a) Open-circuit voltage versus CDs content (10 N, 3 Hz); (b) Frequency-dependent response (1–5 Hz) at 3 wt% CDs under 20 N; (c) Force sensitivity (10–30 N) at 3 wt% CDs (3 Hz); (d) Potential distribution of PVDF;(e) Potential distribution of PVDF/CDs; (f) d33 coefficients across CDs concentrations; (g) Cyclic stability (1,000 cycles) with fatigue profile; (h) Voltage output comparison with CNT/GP-based systems; (i) Macroscopic fiber morphology (3 wt% CDs/CNT/GP).

Practical application of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor: (a) Schematic diagram of connection with capacitor; (b) Charging diagram for small light bulbs; (c) Power supply curves for capacitors of different capacities;(d) TENG connection small light bulb physical picture; (e) Physical drawings of lighting small bulbs; (f) Bending diagrams at different angles and piezoelectric outputs; (g) Piezoelectric output during arm bending, wrist bending and finger pressing.

head.ce:title: Carbon Dot-Driven phase transition in coaxial Wet-Spun PVDF fibers for wearable piezoelectric energy harvesting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flexible piezoelectrics are pivotal for self-powered wearable systems, yet low-dimensional materials like PVDF fibers suffer from inefficient β-phase crystallization and limited charge transport. Here, coaxial wet-spun polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) fibers integrated with carbon quantum dots (CDs) overcome these limitations via solvent-nonsolvent phase separation and mechanical stretching. A 3 wt% CDs doping induces 75.96 % β-phase content through interfacial hydrogen bonding, achieving a high piezoelectric coefficient (d33 of 28 pC/N) and output performance of 19.4 V and 0.2 μA, which is 86.7 % surpassing pristine PVDF(15 pC/N), and Under optimal experimental conditions, the peak voltage and current can reach up to 31 V and 0.2 μA. Cyclic durability tests (3 Hz, 20 N, 1000 cycles) confirm excellent stability with > 90 % performance retention. Practical applications highlight rapid capacitor charging (5 V in 30 s) and the ability to power nine LEDs. Additionally, bending sensitivity (3.6 V at 60°) and motion sensing (9 V for arm bending) validate its adaptability for wearable electronics and IoT applications, offering a scalable strategy for high-performance flexible energy harvesters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) and smart electronics has created growing demand for flexible, self-powered sensors in wearable health monitoring, environmental sensing, and human–machine interfaces [1,2]. To achieve autonomous operation, such devices require efficient ambient energy harvesting solutions to minimize battery dependence and reduce maintenance costs [3–12]. Piezoelectric nanogenerators (PENGs) offer a promising solution by converting mechanical energy into electricity [13,14], Among available piezoelectric materials, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) stands out for PENGs due to its flexibility, high piezoelectric coefficient (d33), and processability [15,16]. Its piezoelectricity stems from the β-phase crystal structure, where optimally aligned molecular dipoles generate strong polarization [17]. However, conventional PVDF has limited β-phase content, which fundamentally constrains its energy conversion efficiency [18].

Despite these inherent limitations, PVDF-based composites continue to attract extensive attention due to their unique combination of flexibility, ease of fabrication, and relatively high performance. Recent advances in functional materials, such as WO3-polyaniline composites [19] and oxide-based materials like ferrites [20], have shown significant potential for energy conversion and storage. Ferrites, in particular, have been found to offer superior stability and lower electromagnetic losses compared to traditional alloys and phosphides [21]. These materials illustrate the diverse strategies being explored in composite research. Within this broader materials context, the targeted enhancement of PVDF's piezoelectric performance through β-phase crystallinity optimization remains a key priority for developing highly effective and flexible energy harvesting systems [22].

To enhance the piezoelectric performance of PVDF, strategies like mechanical stretching, thermal/electric field polarization, and nanofiller doping have been employed to boost β-phase formation [23]. For instance, Shen et al. improved chain alignment via folding-hot-pressing, enhancing both the mechanical and piezoelectric properties [24], and Ong et al. optimized low-temperature phase transitions for energy harvesting [25]. While nanofillers have proven effective in promoting β-phase nucleation [26], significant practical limitations remain: mechanical stretching often induces microstructural defects, thermal/electrical poling methods are energy-intensive [27], and bulk PVDF films lack breathability for wearables applications [28,29]. Nanofiller systems present additional challenges − ZnO nanoparticles tend to aggregate and create defect sites [30], hydrophilic cellulose exhibits poor interfacial bonding with PVDF. Crucially, conventional nanofillers lack sufficient conductivity for efficient charge transport [31,32].

Carbon dots (CDs) present an innovative solution to these challenges, offering exceptional dispersibility, polar surface functional groups (e.g., −OH/−COOH), and intrinsic conductivity [33]. Xu et al. demonstrated CDs’ dual role by decorating ZnO@CDs in PVDF-HFP, achieving 80.4 % β-phase and a 71.6 % higher d33 (33.8 pC/N) [34]. Beyond nanofiller engineering, morphological control has emerged as a powerful strategy for performance optimization. Abolhasani et al. developed porous PVDF-TrFE fibers through phase separation, realizing a 280-fold increase in power output compared to conventional films [35]. However, electrospinning techniques often suffer from inconsistent pore distribution and require high-voltage operation [36,37]. Coaxial wet-spinning addresses these limitations by enabling scalable, cost-effective fiber production with precisely controlled porosity [38]. This technique achieves exceptional structural uniformity through well-regulated solvent-nonsolvent phase separation mechanisms [39,40], overcoming the pore distribution challenges associated with electrospinning while demonstrating excellent potential for industrial-scale manufacturing. The synergistic combination of CDs' unique properties with the structural advantages of wet-spun fibers establishes a promising platform for developing next-generation PVDF-based piezoelectric composites.

This study proposes a novel preparation method for PVDF composite fibers by combining coaxial wet-spinning with the synergistic effects of CDs. Wet-spinning induces the ordered orientation of PVDF molecular chains through the dual effects of solvent-nonsolvent phase separation and mechanical stretching, significantly enhancing the structural stability and piezoelectric performance of the fibers. Experimental results demonstrate that PVDF/CDs fibers with 3 wt% CDs achieve a β-phase content of 75.96 %, a piezoelectric coefficient d 33 of 28 pC/N, and maximum output voltage and current of 19.4 V and 0.2 μA, respectively. The composite also exhibits excellent mechanical flexibility, cyclic stability, and energy harvesting capabilities in wearable devices and self-powered sensors. This work provides fundamental insights into structure-performance relationships in polymer composites and represents a significant advancement toward practical implementation of self-powered IoT systems.

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) pellets (Kynar 705, Shanghai Maiwei Plastic Co., Ltd.), the carbon quantum dots (CDs) were provided by a collaborating lab [41], graphene (GP, 99 % purity, Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd.), and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNT, 99 % purity, Shanghai UniBio Biotechnology Co., Ltd.) were used as raw materials. The solvent N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, analytical grade) was supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., and deionized water (Milli-Q Plus system, 18.2 MΩ·cm) was used as the coagulation medium.

The outer spinning solution was optimized by dissolving 2 g of PVDF pellets in DMF under continuous heating at 60 °C. A predetermined amount of CDs was incorporated, followed by mechanical stirring for 3 h. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to ensure homogeneous dispersion of the CDs. The prepared outer PVDF/CDs solution and inner deionized water were loaded into separate 10 mL syringe barrels and co-extruded through a coaxial needle assembly into a deionized water coagulation bath. The key spinning parameters were set as follows: an outer solution extrusion rate of 4 mL/h, an inner fluid extrusion rate of 2 mL/h, a fiber drawing distance of 100 cm, and a winding speed of 3 rad/min. The as-spun fibers were subsequently immersed in deionized water for 24 h to remove residual solvent and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h to obtain the final PVDF/CDs composite fibers.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-8100) was conducted using a cold field emission instrument at an acceleration voltage of 15 kV. Cross-sectional samples were prepared by liquid nitrogen fracture, mounted with conductive adhesive, and sputter-coated with gold prior to imaging. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was performed using a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer with a scanning range of 500–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation, employing continuous scanning at a step size of 0.004° over a 2θ range of 10°-60°. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were carried out using a DSC instrument with 0.5 mg samples under a nitrogen atmosphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, with full-scan spectra recorded at a pass energy of 100 eV with a step size of 1 eV, and narrow-scan spectra at 50 eV pass energy with a step size of 0.1 eV. Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) data were collected using beamline BL16B1 with an X-ray wavelength of 0.124 nm. Two-dimensional patterns were recorded using a Mar165 CCD detector (2048 × 2048 pixels) at a sample-detector distance of 172 mm. Mechanical properties were tested using an Instron-5967 universal testing machine with fiber samples (initial length: 20 mm) stretched at a rate of 40 mm/min. Five replicates were measured, and average values were calculated. Piezoelectric performance was evaluated using a custom-built testing system with 3 cm fiber samples connected via copper wires. Open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, and charge output were measured using an electrostatic meter and recorded with a digital multimeter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and Packaging of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Piezoelectric sensors were constructed using 3 cm-long PVDF/CDs composite fibers. Copper wires (diameter: 0.2 mm) were used as inner and outer electrodes. The inner electrode was fully inserted into the fiber's hollow core and the outer electrode was adhered to the fiber sheath using conductive epoxy. Insulating adhesive was applied to encapsulate the assembly, ensuring electrical isolation while maintaining mechanical flexibility. The final packaged sensor structure is illustrated in Fig. S1.

A custom-built vertical contact-separation testing system was employed to evaluate piezoelectric performance, comprising two integrated subsystems. One is force application unit: A stepper motor-driven linear stage (travel range: 0–50 mm) enabled precise control of compression frequency (1–5 Hz) and displacement via screw-slide mechanics. Another one is mechanical sensing unit: A calibrated load cell (10–30 N range, ±0.1 N accuracy) monitored real-time pressure profiles during cyclic compression. Piezoelectric outputs were recorded using a high-impedance electrometer (Keithley 6514) coupled with a graphical sampling multimeter, ensuring synchronous acquisition of voltage/current signals under dynamic loading conditions.

COMSOL Multiphysics is based on finite element method (FEM) to analyze the coupling of physical models at different scales. We carried out piezoelectric simulation of the model by coupling electrostatic field and solid mechanics. Two differense-set cylinders were used as hollow pvdf (inner diameter = 95 nm, outer diameter = 200 nm, length = 1000 nm), and 46 carbon quantum dots were set up and introduced into the material. On this basis, the geometric model is established, and then the finite element mesh is generated. The complete grid contains 1,506,481 domain units,96496 boundary units, and 6269 edge units.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of PVDF/CDs composite fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the formal experiments, the ratio between the outer PVDF solution and the inner deionized water was optimized, as illustrated in Fig. S2. As shown in the figure, the highest piezoelectric output was achieved at a PVDF-to-deionized water ratio of 4:3. Therefore, this ratio was adopted for all subsequent experiments.

Fig. 1 illustrates the wet spinning process to prepare PVDF/CDS composite fibers. During this process, the bidirectional diffusion mechanism between the solvent and the coagulation bath plays a critical role in promoting the formation of the β-phase in PVDF [42]. The mutual diffusion of the spinning solution and the coagulation bath rapidly alters the chemical environment, reducing the solubility of PVDF. As a result, PVDF molecular chains precipitate at the solvent-nonsolvent interface and form a solid phase [43]. Since water in the coagulation bath has extremely low solubility for PVDF, the PVDF chains align in an all-trans (TTTT) conformation, which significantly promotes the formation of highly polarized β-phase crystals [44].

The incorporation of CDs further induces the ordered arrangement of PVDF molecular chains. Due to their high electronegativity [45], CDs generate localized electrostatic fields within the PVDF matrix, influencing the surrounding molecular environment and promoting the alignment of PVDF chains along a specific orientation. Under these electrostatic fields, the dipole moments of fluorine and hydrogen atoms within the PVDF chains are effectively polarized. Furthermore, the functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, interact with the fluorine and hydrogen atoms in PVDF chains through hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions [46]. These interactions enable CDs to act as nucleation centers within the PVDF matrix [47], facilitating the realignment of molecular chains and enhancing their overall orientation and polarization, thereby significantly accelerating the formation of the β-phase.

It is worth noting that such enhancement mechanisms, involving interfacial polarization and improved charge transfer efficiency, are broadly applicable and have been commonly observed in various multiphase polymer composite systems. For example, Ga-doped barium ferrite composites have demonstrated significantly enhanced dielectric and piezoelectric performance, attributed to similar interface-driven polarization and minimized recombination losses [48,49]. Although these specific composite systems differ from our PVDF/CDs material, their underlying enhancement mechanisms clearly illustrate the general relevance and significance of interfacial interactions in optimizing the properties of composite materials.

During the spinning process, the combined effects of mechanical drawing forces in the coagulation bath and interfacial interactions further stretch the preliminarily aligned molecular chains, ensuring a more consistent chain orientation and stabilizing the β-phase formation. Simultaneously, CDs redistribute in the matrix alongside the movement of PVDF chains during spinning and stretching. Given their nanoscale dimensions, CDs are uniformly dispersed in the PVDF matrix during the drawing process, minimizing aggregation and forming a stable and uniform nanocomposite structure. This uniform distribution further enhances the composite material's performance by increasing the β-phase content and improving its piezoelectric response.

The SEM images of PVDF composite fibers with varying CDs contents (0–4 wt%, Fig. 2 a-2c, Fig. S3) clearly demonstrate significant morphological changes and enhanced β-phase formation, both induced by CDs incorporation. Cross-sectional images (Fig. S3(a1-a5)) demonstrate that increasing CDs concentrations progressively transform the fiber morphology from a relatively compact structure to one with enhanced porosity and uniform pore distribution. This structural evolution is attributed to interactions between CDs and PVDF molecular chains. Medium-magnification images (Fig. S3(b1-b5)) confirm that CDs incorporation markedly increases pore density and improves pore uniformity, driven by hydrogen bonding interactions between polar functional groups (–OH, –COOH) on CDs surfaces and fluorine/hydrogen atoms in PVDF chains. These interactions promote the reorganization of molecular chains during the fiber formation process, thereby leading to changes in the pore size of the fibers. High-magnification images (Fig. S3(c1-c5)) emphasize this structural refinement. At 3 wt% CDs content, fibers exhibit exceptionally ordered micropore architectures. The high electronegativity of CDs induces localized electrostatic fields within the PVDF matrix, synergizing with hydrogen bonding to promote alignment of PVDF chains. This dual mechanism of electrostatic polarization from CDs electronegativity combined with hydrogen-bond-directed molecular ordering enhances the formation and stabilization of the electroactive β-phase. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CDs serve as anchoring sites for PVDF chains through hydrogen bonding, restricting random chain motion and favoring extended conformations conducive to β-phase crystallization. This interfacial interaction mechanism explains the observed correlation between CDs content, structural ordering, and enhanced β-phase content.

To quantitatively analyze the effect of CDs doping on the β-phase content variation in PVDF, the β-phase fraction of PVDF/CDs composite fibers was calculated using the Beer-Lambert law (Fig. S4). The proportion of beta phase (F (β)) in the sample can be determined using Lambert-Beer's law based on the results obtained from FTIR using the following equation [50]:
F
β
=
X β
X α
+
X β
=
A β
1.26
A α
+
A α
× 100 %
Where, Xα and Xβ are the crystal mass fraction of α and β phases, respectively, and Aα and Aβ correspond to the absorbance intensity at 760 cm−1 and 840 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, FTIR spectral profiles (Fig. 2d) jointly revealed the influence of CDs doping on β-phase formation in PVDF, highlighting significant structural transitions with varying CDs concentrations. In the undoped sample (0 wt%), PVDF predominantly exhibited characteristic peaks of the nonpolar α-phase, as evidenced by strong absorption bands at 760 cm−1 (CF2 bending vibration of α-phase) and 614 cm−1 (CH2 rocking vibration of α-phase). These intense peaks indicated a predominant α-phase composition, with the β-phase fraction accounting for only 60.12 %. When the CDs concentration increased from 1 wt% to 3 wt%, the intensity of β-phase characteristic peaks at 840 cm−1 (CH2 all-trans conformation vibration) significantly intensified. This transition originated from the robust hydrogen bonding interactions between the abundant polar functional groups (–OH/–COOH) on CDs surfaces and the C-F bonds in PVDF molecular chains, which forced the molecular chains to transform from random coils (α-phase) to all-trans conformations (β-phase). Consequently, the β-phase fraction reached a maximum of 75.96 %. However, when the doping level further increased to 4 wt%, the β-phase content decreased to 72.47 %, attributed to excessive CDs agglomerates that hindered the mobility of molecular chains and disrupted the continuity of the hydrogen-bonding network.

XRD analysis (Fig. 2e) further confirms these findings. The β-phase characteristic peak at approximately 20° (2θ) becomes more pronounced as CDs content increases from 0 wt% to 3 wt%, demonstrating that CDs effectively induce a transition to the highly polarized β-phase. In contrast, the α-phase peak at approximately 18.4° gradually diminishes, indicating that CDs suppress the formation of the non-polar α-phase. At CDs content of 4 wt%, the β-phase peak weakens due to CDs aggregation, which reduces surface effects, disrupts uniform dispersion, and introduces uneven stress fields in the PVDF matrix, thereby inhibiting molecular chain alignment and β-phase formation.

In order to clarify the influence mechanism of carbon quantum dots (CDs) on the crystallization kinetics and phase transition behavior of PVDF wet-spun fibers, the influence on the thermal behavior of PVDF is shown in the Fig. 2f. The significant melting peak in DSC can reflect the existence of different crystal phases and the change of thermal properties in PVDF. PVDF samples (0 wt%) with undoped carbon quantum dots show a distinct melting peak at about 160 °C, representing a crystalline structure dominated by alpha phase. However, with the increase of carbon quantum dot doping concentration, especially at 1 wt%, 2 wt% and 3 wt%, the melting peak of DSC gradually moves to high temperature, and the shape of the melting peak also changes, which indicates a significant change in the crystalline phase structure of PVDF [51]. When the concentration of carbon quantum dots increased to 4 wt%, the melting peak showed a slight downward trend, and the peak shape was not as obvious as that at 3 wt%. This may be due to the aggregation effect of carbon quantum dots at higher concentrations, which leads to poor uniform distribution in the matrix, thus inhibiting the effective formation of PVDF-β phase. Therefore, through DSC analysis, it can be seen that carbon quantum dots can effectively improve the content and stability of β phase in PVDF when the right amount (such as 3 wt%).

WAXS analysis further demonstrates the influence of varying CDs concentrations on the crystalline structure and crystallization behavior of PVDF/CDs composites. In the two-dimensional WAXS diffraction patterns (Fig. S5), the brightness and sharpness of the diffraction rings associated with the PVDF crystalline phase significantly improve as the CDs content increases, especially at concentrations from 1 wt% to 3 wt%. This indicates a notable increase in crystallinity and structural order. However, at 4 wt%, the brightness of the diffraction rings weakens, suggesting that excessive CDs negatively affect PVDF crystallization behavior, leading to a decline in crystalline order. Correspondingly, the one-dimensional diffraction intensity curve (Fig. 2g) shows that the observed peaks at 20.4° correspond to the (110) and (200) lattice reflections of the β phase, while the diffraction peaks at 17.8° and 18.5° correspond to the (100/020) and (110) plane reflections of the α phase [52] The intensity of β-phase characteristic peaks increases with the increase of CDs content, with a peak value of 3 wt%, which reflects optimal crystallization and polarization. At 4 wt%, the characteristic peak of β phase decreased, indicating that excess CD may interfere with molecular chain arrangement and crystallization, thereby reducing β phase content. These results underscore that appropriate CDs doping enhances β-phase formation and piezoelectric performance, while excessive doping can compromise structural stability and crystalline order.

XPS analysis confirms the successful integration of CDs into PVDF matrices. Quantitative elemental profiles (Table. S1) reveal increased C (52.34 % → 52.14 %), N (1.40 → 2.05 %), and O (12.94 % →15.42 %) content in the composite, directly evidencing CDs incorporation. High-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2i, Fig. S6c) demonstrate critical differences: pristine PVDF exhibits only CC (284.8 eV) and C-F (290.5 eV) bonds, while the 3 wt% CDs-PVDF composite shows an additional peak at 286.6 eV, assigned to hydrogen-bonded interactions between PVDF’s C-F groups and CDs’ oxygen/nitrogen functionalities (–COOH, –OH). This interfacial bonding is further supported by O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2h, Fig. S6b): pure PVDF displays a single COC peak (532.4 eV), whereas the composite resolves three components of COC (532.4 eV), C-OH (533.3 eV), and O-F (535.1 eV), with the latter two peaks uniquely arising from hydrogen bonds (C-F···HO) at the PVDF-CDs interface. These hydrogen bonds act as molecular anchors, aligning PVDF chains into polarized β-phase conformations and reducing charge recombination losses. The synergistic interplay between CD-induced polarization and structural ordering underpins the composite’s enhanced piezoelectric output.

The stress–strain curves (Fig. S7) indicate that increasing the CDs content significantly enhances the tensile strength and elongation of the fibers. Optimal mechanical performance is observed at 3 wt% CDs doping, where the tensile strength reaches approximately 12 MPa, accompanied by markedly enhanced elongation. This improvement is mainly attributed to the strong interfacial interactions between CDs and the PVDF matrix, which effectively reinforce the composite structure and thus enhance its mechanical robustness. However, at a higher doping concentration of 4 wt%, the mechanical properties slightly decline, primarily due to CDs aggregation causing increased stress concentration effects.

In addition to mechanical characteristics, the microstructural uniformity and grain size within composite fibers also significantly influence their electronic and piezoelectric properties. Generally, a finer grain structure increases the surface-to-volume ratio, which enhances interfacial polarization and dielectric performance. Nevertheless, excessively fine microstructures may impede effective charge transport, potentially leading to higher recombination losses [53]. Therefore, achieving precise control over grain size and structural uniformity is essential for optimizing both the mechanical robustness and electrical functionality of the composite fibers.

Practical tensile tests (Fig. S8a) further validate the fibers' capability to withstand mechanical loads ranging from 5 g to 50 g without significant deformation, with 3 wt% CDs fibers displaying optimal load-bearing capacity. Moreover, weaving tests (Fig. S8b) demonstrate excellent flexibility and processability, confirming that these fibers can be easily woven into various shapes without compromising structural integrity. Such features render these fibers highly suitable for flexible electronics and wearable device applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the piezoelectric performance of PVDF/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to observe the effect of piezoelectric material selection on the piezoelectric output performance, the piezoelectric performance of pure PVDF fiber and PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor with different CDs content was tested and compared. In this test, the force is applied at 10 N and the frequency is 3 Hz. The experimental results show that the concentration of CDs plays a key role in the piezoelectric properties of wet-spun PVDF/CDs composite fibers. This is shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S9a, the output voltage and current both show a CDs-dependent enhancement, reaching maximum values of ∼ 19.4 V and ∼ 0.2 μA, respectively, at a load of 3 wt% CDs. The output voltage of undoped PVDF (0 wt% CDs) fiber is 0.72 V, which is nearly 26 times higher. This confirms the important role of CDs incorporation. This enhancement is mainly due to the hydrogen bond interaction between the oxygen-containing groups (–OH, –COOH) on the CD and PVDF chains, which promotes molecular arrangement and β phase crystallization. Hydrogen bond oriented recombination of PVDF chains contributes to dipole orientation and charge separation efficiency. However, CDs content of more than 3 wt% resulted in decreased performance (Fig. 3a), which may be due to the aggregation of CDs destroying the hydrogen bond network in the PVDF matrix and causing structural heterogeneity. Similar studies have shown that in graphene /PVDF composites, aggregation of fillers will form an independent phase region, and there is a significant interface and polarization discontinuity between the filler and the matrix, thus aggravating the charge recombination probability [52]. This clustering effect is the common cause of performance degradation in both systems.

The PVDF/CDs-3 wt% piezoelectric sensor with the best medium voltage performance was selected to study the influence of frequency on the piezoelectric output performance of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor. Under the condition that the applied pressure is set to 20 N, the open circuit voltage and short circuit current of the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor are compared by changing the frequency (1 Hz, 2 Hz, 3 Hz, 4 Hz, 5 Hz), as shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S9b. As the frequency increases, the output voltage and current increase synchronously and reach a peak value (27 V, 0.16 μA) at 4–5 Hz, indicating that the gradually increasing frequency vibration accelerates molecular chain polarization and charge accumulation (Fig. 3b). This property highlights the material's potential for low-frequency vibration energy harvesting (such as human movement or ambient vibration) and wide-band mechanical stress detection. Compared to traditional PVDF/ZnO/BaTiO3 composites [54] (4 Hz peak voltage only 11 V), increased the voltage output 145 %, and without external polarization efficient electromechanical conversion can be realized.

The piezoelectric output of the PVDF/CDs-3 wt% sensor was evaluated under an applied force range of 10–30 N at a frequency of 3 Hz. As shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S10c, the output voltage reached 31 V when the external pressure increased to 25 N, accompanied by a synchronous rise in current(0.15 μA), indicating enhanced interfacial polarization and optimized dipole alignment under high-pressure conditions. The piezoelectric output significantly outperformed those of comparable materials, such as MXene/PVDF [55] (6 V at 25 N) and graphene/PVDF (10 V at 25 N) [56] 10 V. Although the growth rate of the piezoelectric output stabilized when the applied force reached 25 N, the material’s superior mechanical robustness still highlights its applicability in high-load scenarios, such as industrial vibration monitoring or wearable devices. Compared to existing systems, the self-polarization capability (eliminating the need for harsh polarization conditions required by PZT) [57] and β-phase stability (75.96 % crystallinity) of this composite demonstrate distinct advantages, providing a novel strategy for developing low-frequency, high-sensitivity piezoelectric devices.

To elucidate the working mechanism of the PVDF/CDs composite fibers, we carried out COMSOL simulation to analyze the piezoelectric potential distribution under mechanical pressure. The direct piezoelectric effect induces charge separation, generating a potential gradient across the material [58]. The potential distributions of pure PVDF and PVDF/CDs composite fibers were evaluated under diametric compressive loading (applied vertically from right to left). The three-dimensional potential maps (Fig. 3d, Fig. 3e) demonstrate the significant enhancement in charge separation capability due to CDs doping. For pure PVDF fibers (Fig. 3d), the potential range was narrow (−0.0128 V to + 0.0137 V), with scattered high-potential regions (red) and shallow, concave low-potential zones (blue), indicating limited charge separation capacity. In contrast, PVDF/CDs composite fibers (Fig. 3e) exhibited a substantially wider potential range (−0.0203 V to + 0.0177 V), with the negative potential strength increasing by 58 % and the positive potential strength by 29 %. These simulation results directly correlate with the experimental voltage output of the CDs-doped system, confirming that incorporating carbon quantum dots significantly enhances the piezoelectric properties.

In order to evaluate the piezoelectric properties of PVDF/CDs composite fibers, the longitudinal piezoelectric constant (d33) was measured (Fig. 3f), which directly reflects the coupling efficiency between the mechanical stress and charge response of the material [59]. Compared with traditional and emerging piezoelectric materials, PVDF/CDs-3 wt%composites exhibited superior piezoelectric properties. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the longitudinal piezoelectric coefficient (d33 = 28 pC/N) was 86.6 % higher than that of pure PVDF (15 pC/N), and significantly higher than that of other PVDF composites requiring external polarization. For example, PVDF/graphene (16 pC/N) [60] and PVDF/BaTiO3 (18 pC/N) [61]. This enhancement is attributed to the synergistic effect of tensile stress induced during wet spinning with polar groups (–OH/–COOH) on the CDs surface, which avoids the high-pressure polarization step required by traditional piezoelectric ceramics (such as PZT) by promoting β phase formation (β phase content 75.96 %) and molecular dipole self-alignment. It is worth noting that when the CDs doping amount increased to 4.0 wt%, the d33 value decreased to 26.5 pC/N. The decrease in d3 highlights the importance of optimizing the dispersion of fillers to prevent chain migration restriction and hydrogen bond destruction caused by agglomeration. This is a common challenge for nano-filler reinforced composites. These results position PVDF/CDs as a promising candidate for self-powered wearable sensors and low-frequency energy harvesters, combining ease of handling, environmental compatibility, and strong electromechanical coupling. Future research should focus on scaling production and enhancing interface interactions to mitigate performance degradation under high packing loads.

To clearly assess the piezoelectric stability of PVDF/CDs composite sensors, PVDF fibers incorporating 3 wt% CNT and GP, namely PVDF/CNT and PVDF/GP composite fibers, were fabricated. These fibers were then assembled into piezoelectric sensors and subjected to stability testing. The tests were conducted at a frequency of 3 Hz with an applied force of 20 N. After 1000 cycles, the piezoelectric performance of the PVDF fibers containing CDs, CNT, and GP was compared and analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 3g and Fig. S11, the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor demonstrated highly stable output without significant fluctuations, with the output voltage reaching as high as 20 V, which is notably higher than that of the PVDF/CNT and PVDF/GP sensors. Fig. 3i presents the visual appearance of the three fiber types, where PVDF/CNT and PVDF/GP fibers appear light gray, while the PVDF/CDs composite fiber is white. This color contrast not only highlights the excellent biocompatibility of CDs but also suggests an efficient integration of CDs with PVDF. These findings underscore the superior cyclic stability and piezoelectric output performance of the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensors.

Fig. 3h illustrates the piezoelectric output performance of various composite materials, plotted against the filler mass fraction (wt%) and the corresponding output voltage. From the graph, it is evident that the PVDF/CDs composite fibers (marked with a red star) exhibit significantly higher output voltage compared to the other materials, especially within the filler mass fraction range of 1 to 3 wt%. The PVDF/CDs fibers outperform the CNT and GP-based composites in terms of piezoelectric output, highlighting the superior performance of CDs when incorporated into the PVDF matrix. This elevated output voltage indicates that the PVDF/CDs composite not only enhances piezoelectric performance but also achieves greater efficiency in converting mechanical energy into electrical signals.

To further analyze the energy supply capability of PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensors in real-world environments, the sensor was applied to capacitor charging and lighting experiments to evaluate its power supply efficiency. The working principles are illustrated in Fig. 4 a, Fig. 4b, Fig. 4d and Fig. S10. A 3✕3 cm2 PVDF/CDs composite fiber was fabricated into a piezoelectric sensor, with its piezoelectric output connected to capacitors of varying capacities (1 µF, 2.2 µF, and 3.3 µF) via a full-wave rectifier bridge. The charging curves in Fig. 4c show that the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor can charge a capacitor to 5 V within 30 s, demonstrating excellent power delivery capability. Additionally, the sensor was used to power commercial red LED bulbs to verify its performance in practical scenarios. As shown in Fig. 4e, the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor successfully illuminated nine LED bulbs, confirming its potential for real-world applications.

By arranging PVDF/CDs composite fibers in an orderly manner, a simple sensor (3✕3 cm2 in size) was constructed to monitor electrical signals generated by arm bending, wrist flexion, and finger pressing. As shown in Fig. 4g, the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor demonstrated excellent response speed, producing an output voltage of 0.7 V during rapid finger pressing, while achieving output voltages of 9 V and 6 V during slow arm bending and wrist flexion, respectively. Fig. 4f shows the piezoelectric output performance of the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor when subjected to different bending angles (15°, 30°, 45°, and 60°), with the output voltages reaching 2.7 V, 3.1 V, 3.3 V, and 3.6 V respectively. This result clearly indicates that the output voltage of the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor gradually increases with the increase of the bending angle. This is because as the bending angle expands, more piezoelectric layers located near the bending area are subjected to pressure, thereby promoting the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor to generate a higher voltage output. These results confirm the high efficiency and sensitivity of the PVDF/CDs piezoelectric sensor in capturing and converting mechanical movements into electrical signals.

In summary, we have developed a coaxial wet-spun PVDF/ CDs composite fiber with high voltage output and d33 coefficient. Through synergistic integration of solvent-nonsolvent phase separation, mechanical stretching, and 3 wt% CD doping, the composite achieved a β-phase crystallinity of 75.96 % and enhanced molecular polarization, yielding a piezoelectric coefficient (d33) of 28 pC/N — an 86.7 % improvement over pristine PVDF (15 pC/N) — with concurrent outputs of 19.4 V and 0.2 μA. Under cyclic loading (3 Hz, 20 N, 1000 cycles), the PVDF/CDs sensors retained over 90 % voltage stability at 19.4 V, outperforming PVDF/ CNT and PVDF/ GP composites. Demonstrated energy harvesting utility included rapid capacitor charging (5 V in 30 s) and direct powering of nine LEDs. Angular bending sensitivity tests revealed angle-dependent outputs up to 3.6 V (60° curvature), while wearable motion sensing generated distinct voltages from biomechanical activities: 9 V (arm bending), 6 V (wrist flexion), and 0.7 V (finger compression). These findings highlight CDs' dual role in structural optimization and charge transfer enhancement, providing a scalable fabrication platform for durable self-powered wearables and IoT sensors.

Jun Yu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tiantian Yue: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mengqi Wang: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yi Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Fan Liao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yuqing Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
